content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 44 (GEN_044)

## 1. Nachesh Yenachesh — "Practices Divination" vs. "Read the Hidden Things" (vv. 5, 15)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he indeed practices divination with it" | Infinitive absolute of nachash = divination |
| LOGOS | "by which he indeed practices divination" | Same — technical term preserved |
| PNEUMA | "uses for divination" (v.5) / "can read the hidden things" (v.15) | Dynamic — interprets the divination claim |
| **SOPHIA** | **"indeed practices divination" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | The root nachash specifically means "to practice divination, read omens." PNEUMA's "read the hidden things" is an interpretive paraphrase that loses the technical specificity. The infinitive absolute demands emphatic rendering. |

## 2. Neqiyim — "Blameless" vs. "Free" (v. 10)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall be blameless" | naqiy = clean, blameless, guiltless |
| LOGOS | "you shall be blameless" | Same — judicial quality preserved |
| PNEUMA | "the rest of you will be free" | Dynamic — implies physical release |
| **SOPHIA** | **"blameless" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | Hebrew naqiy is a legal-moral term meaning "clean, guiltless, innocent." "Free" implies physical release; "blameless" preserves the judicial quality of the steward's verdict: the brothers are declared cleared of guilt, not merely released. |

## 3. Avon — "Iniquity" vs. "Guilt" (v. 16)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the iniquity of your servants" | avon = moral weight of accumulated wrongdoing |
| LOGOS | "the iniquity of your servants" | Same — theological register preserved |
| PNEUMA | "the guilt of your servants" | More accessible English |
| **SOPHIA** | **"iniquity" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | Avon is the heaviest word in the Hebrew moral vocabulary — from a root meaning "to be bent, twisted." It denotes not just guilt but the accumulated weight and distortion of wrongdoing. "Guilt" captures the consequence but not the nature of the sin. In Judah's confession, the deeper theological weight of avon should be preserved. |

## 4. Bera'ah vs. Beyagon — "In Evil" vs. "In Grief" (vv. 29, 31)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "in evil" (v.29) / "in grief" (v.31) | Preserves distinction: ra'ah ≠ yagon |
| LOGOS | "in evil" (v.29) / "in sorrow" (v.31) | Preserves distinction: ra'ah ≠ yagon |
| PNEUMA | "in grief" (v.29) / "in grief" (v.31) | Conflates both terms |
| **SOPHIA** | **"in evil" / "in sorrow" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew uses two distinct words: ra'ah (evil, disaster, calamity) in v. 29 and yagon (sorrow, grief) in v. 31. PNEUMA conflates them both as "grief." The distinction matters: v. 29 describes the quality of the catastrophe; v. 31 describes the emotional state. SOPHIA preserves the distinction: "in evil" (v. 29), "in sorrow" (v. 31). |

## 5. Nephesh — "Soul" vs. "Life" (v. 30)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his soul is bound up with his soul" | nephesh = whole self, soul, breath |
| LOGOS | "his soul is bound up with the boy's soul" | Same + clarified referent |
| PNEUMA | "his life is bound up with the boy's life" | nephesh as "life" — accessible |
| **SOPHIA** | **"his soul is bound up with the boy's soul" — LOGOS upheld** | Nephesh means the whole animated self. "Soul" captures the intertwining of Jacob's entire being with Benjamin's — something deeper than mere physical survival. LOGOS's clarification of the pronoun referent ("with the boy's soul") prevents ambiguity. |

## 6. Na'ar — "Youth" vs. "Boy" (v. 33 and throughout)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the youth" | na'ar = youth, young man |
| LOGOS | "the boy" | More natural English for the context |
| PNEUMA | "the boy" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the boy" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | Consistent with the convention established in Gen 42:22, where "boy" was chosen over "child" for a young man. Benjamin is now likely in his early twenties. "Boy" is more natural in English while still conveying youth and vulnerability. |

---

*Genesis 44: 6 disagreements logged. SOPHIA ruled with EMET/LOGOS on 3 items (vv. 5/15, 10, 16), with LOGOS alone on 2 items (vv. 29/31 distinction, v. 30), and with LOGOS/PNEUMA on 1 item (v. 33). This was a chapter of exceptionally high agreement — all three translators aligned on the major theological and literary moments: the garment-tearing echo (v. 13), the triple rhetorical question (v. 16), the divinely attributed confession, Judah's entire speech structure (vv. 18-34), the "my wife bore me two" statement (v. 27), the tarof toraf echo (v. 28), and the climactic self-substitution with tachat (v. 33). The disagreements center on register choices and English idiom rather than interpretation or theology. The moral and narrative power of Judah's great speech was rendered with remarkable consistency across all three translators.*
